title: Microsoft's MDASH Agentic System Discovers 16 Windows Vulnerabilities
summary: Microsoft's new multi-model agentic security harness (codename MDASH) found 16 new vulnerabilities in Windows, including four critical remote code execution flaws, and topped the CyberGym industry benchmark.
content: Microsoft announced that its new agentic security system, codename MDASH, orchestrates over 100 specialized AI agents across an ensemble of frontier and distilled models to discover, debate, and prove exploitable bugs end-to-end. The system helped researchers find 16 new vulnerabilities across the Windows networking and authentication stack—including four Critical remote code execution flaws in components such as the Windows kernel TCP/IP stack and the IKEv2 service. MDASH achieved an industry-leading 88.45% score on the public CyberGym benchmark of 1,507 real-world vulnerabilities, roughly five points ahead of the next entry, and showed 96% recall on five years of Microsoft Security Response Center cases in clfs.sys and 100% recall in tcpip.sys. Microsoft says the durable advantage lies in the agentic system around the model—not any single model itself—making the harness model-agnostic and upgradeable as new frontier AI models arrive. The system is currently used by Microsoft security engineering teams and is entering a limited private preview for select customers.

title: Google Stops First Confirmed AI-Developed Zero-Day Exploit
summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group detected and disrupted a zero-day exploit it says was developed with AI assistance, marking the first confirmed instance of AI-authored malware caught in the wild.
content: Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) reported stopping a zero-day exploit that 'prominent cyber crime threat actors' were planning to deploy in a mass exploitation event targeting the two-factor authentication system of an unnamed open-source web administration tool. Researchers identified telltale signs of AI authorship in the Python exploit script, including a 'hallucinated CVSS score' and 'structured, textbook' formatting consistent with large language model training data. Although Google says it does not believe its own Gemini models were used, it has high confidence that an AI model was involved in both discovering the vulnerability and weaponizing the exploit. The report also highlights a growing trend of attackers using 'persona-driven jailbreaking'—prompting AI to roleplay as security experts—to extract vulnerability information, and feeding models entire repositories of CVE data. Google analyst John Hultquist described the find as 'the tip of the iceberg' and a sign that AI will increasingly be weaponized by threat actors at scale.

title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak to Counter Anthropic's Project Glasswing
summary: OpenAI unveiled Daybreak, a cybersecurity AI initiative combining GPT-5.5-Cyber and Codex Security to proactively detect and patch software vulnerabilities before attackers can exploit them.
content: OpenAI launched Daybreak, positioning it as its answer to Anthropic's Claude Mythos-based Project Glasswing, with the goal of proactively finding and patching vulnerabilities before attackers do. Daybreak uses the Codex Security AI agent—released in March—to build threat models from an organization's codebase, map potential attack paths, validate likely vulnerabilities, and automate detection of the highest-risk issues. The initiative is not built on a single model; OpenAI says 'Daybreak brings together the most capable OpenAI models, Codex, and our security partners,' including the specialized GPT-5.5 with Trusted Access for Cyber and GPT-5.5-Cyber, which began rolling out the prior week. The launch comes just over a month after Anthropic announced Claude Mythos as too dangerous for public release, signaling that AI-powered offensive and defensive cybersecurity is becoming a major competitive battleground among frontier AI labs. OpenAI also said it is working with industry and government partners as it prepares to deploy increasingly capable cyber-focused models.

title: Baidu's ERNIE 5.1 Cuts AI Training Costs by 94%
summary: Baidu released ERNIE 5.1, a frontier-class AI model that cost roughly 94% less to train than comparable systems by compressing its predecessor's architecture rather than building from scratch.
content: Baidu officially released ERNIE 5.1, claiming pre-training costs of just 6% of what comparable frontier models typically spend—achieved through a technique called 'multi-dimensional elastic pre-training.' Instead of training from scratch, Baidu extracted an optimized sub-network from ERNIE 5.0, compressing total parameters to about one-third and active parameters by half while retaining the parent model's full knowledge base. On LMArena Search Arena, where real users rank models on live web search tasks by human preference, ERNIE 5.1 scored 1,223—fourth globally and first among all Chinese models—and its agentic capabilities surpassed DeepSeek-V4-Pro. The post-training pipeline employs a four-stage reinforcement learning system called MOPD (Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation), which trains specialist expert models in code, reasoning, and agentic tasks in parallel before distilling them into a single unified model to avoid 'seesaw effects' where improving one skill tanks another. On AIME26, the model scored 99.6% with tool-assisted reasoning, trailing only Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Baidu showcased further industrial applications at its Create 2026 developer conference in Beijing.

title: Google, Microsoft, and xAI Agree to Pre-Release Government Safety Testing
summary: Google, Microsoft, and xAI have voluntarily agreed to submit new AI models to the US Department of Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation for safety evaluation before public release.
content: The US Department of Commerce announced that Google, Microsoft, and xAI have agreed to voluntarily submit their new AI models and capabilities for testing through the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) before releasing them to the public. The pacts expand on similar agreements reached with OpenAI and Anthropic during the Biden Administration, extending pre-release evaluations covering capabilities and security to a broader set of frontier AI developers. CAISI director Chris Fall stated that 'these expanded industry collaborations help us scale our work in the public interest at a critical moment,' noting the center has already conducted 40 previous evaluations including testing of unreleased state-of-the-art models. The move marks a notable shift for the Trump administration, which has generally favored a hands-off approach to AI regulation, but appears to be recalibrating as the US military expands AI use and Anthropic's disclosure of Claude Mythos—deemed too dangerous to release publicly—raised alarm across government agencies. Microsoft acknowledged in a blog post that 'testing for national security and large-scale public safety risks necessarily must be a collaborative endeavour with governments.'

title: OpenAI Replaces ChatGPT's Default Model with GPT-5.5 Instant
summary: OpenAI replaced GPT-5.3 Instant with GPT-5.5 Instant as ChatGPT's default model for all users, delivering 52.5% fewer hallucinations and enhanced personalization through memory and past-chat context.
content: OpenAI rolled out GPT-5.5 Instant as the new default model for all ChatGPT users, replacing GPT-5.3 Instant globally across free and paid tiers. In internal evaluations, GPT-5.5 Instant produced 52.5% fewer hallucinated claims than its predecessor on high-stakes prompts covering medicine, law, and finance, and reduced inaccurate claims by 37.3% on conversations users had flagged for factual errors. The model delivers tighter, less verbose responses without sacrificing substance, and is smarter about deciding when to invoke web search to improve its answers. Alongside the model update, OpenAI introduced 'memory sources'—a transparency layer that shows users exactly which saved memories, past chats, or connected files (such as Gmail) informed a personalized response, with full controls to delete or correct that context. Enhanced personalization is rolling out first to Plus and Pro subscribers on the web, with plans to expand to Free, Go, Business, and Enterprise users in the coming weeks, while GPT-5.3 Instant remains accessible for paid users for three more months before retirement.
